nodes.label: Thales
nodes.description: The first philosopher in the Western tradition. Asked what the world is fundamentally made of. The birth of rational inquiry.

nodes.label: Heraclitus
nodes.description: The philosopher of flux and the Logos. You cannot step into the same river twice. Reality is perpetual change.

nodes.label: Parmenides
nodes.description: The philosopher of Being. Argued reality is one, unchanging, and eternal — change is illusion.

nodes.label: Pythagoras
nodes.description: Mathematician and mystic. Discovered that mathematical ratios govern musical harmony.

nodes.label: Democritus
nodes.description: The founder of atomism. Reality consists entirely of indivisible particles moving through void.

nodes.label: Socrates
nodes.description: The father of Western moral philosophy. Turned philosophy from cosmology to ethics. Executed for his ideas.

nodes.label: Plato
nodes.description: Student of Socrates, teacher of Aristotle. His theory of Forms established the split between appearance and reality.

nodes.label: Aristotle
nodes.description: The philosopher. Grounded philosophy in observable reality. Founded logic, biology, physics, ethics, politics.

nodes.label: Epicurus
nodes.description: Founded Epicureanism — pursuit of tranquil pleasure through friendship and freedom from fear.

nodes.label: Zeno of Citium
nodes.description: Founder of Stoicism. Virtue is the only good. Focus on what is within your control.

nodes.label: Pyrrho
nodes.description: Founder of ancient Skepticism. We cannot know anything with certainty.

nodes.label: Cicero
nodes.description: Rome greatest philosopher. Translated Greek concepts into Latin. Founded natural law theory.

nodes.label: Marcus Aurelius
nodes.description: Roman Emperor and Stoic philosopher. His Meditations remain the definitive statement of Stoic practice.

nodes.label: Plotinus
nodes.description: Founder of Neoplatonism. Reality emanates from the One through Intellect and Soul to matter.

nodes.label: Laozi
nodes.description: Author of the Tao Te Ching and founder of Taoism. The Tao cannot be named, only lived in harmony with.

nodes.label: Confucius
nodes.description: The most influential philosopher in Chinese history. System of social ethics shaped Chinese civilization for 2,500 years.

nodes.label: Mozi
nodes.description: Founder of Mohism. Advocated universal love and utilitarian calculation. Anticipates Bentham by 2,000 years.

nodes.label: Zhuangzi
nodes.description: The literary genius of Taoism. Philosophy of radical perspectivism challenges all dogmatic systems.

nodes.label: Mencius
nodes.description: The second Confucian sage. Argued human nature is fundamentally good.

nodes.label: Han Fei
nodes.description: Greatest philosopher of Legalism. Only clear laws and strict enforcement produce social order. Anticipates Machiavelli.

nodes.label: Upanishadic Sages
nodes.description: The sages whose dialogues constitute the Upanishads. Atman is identical to Brahman — the most radical assertion of consciousness over matter.

nodes.label: Al-Kindi
nodes.description: The first philosopher in the Arabic tradition. Reconciled Greek philosophy with Islamic theology.

nodes.label: Al-Farabi
nodes.description: The Second Teacher after Aristotle. Most comprehensive synthesis of Aristotelian philosophy and Islamic thought.

nodes.label: Avicenna (Ibn Sina)
nodes.description: Greatest philosopher-physician of medieval world. His floating man thought experiment anticipates Descartes.

nodes.label: Al-Ghazali
nodes.description: The philosopher who attacked philosophy. His victory over rationalism is one of history most consequential intellectual defeats.

nodes.label: Augustine of Hippo
nodes.description: Synthesized Neoplatonism with Christian theology. City of God shaped Western political thought for a millennium.

nodes.label: Thomas Aquinas
nodes.description: Greatest medieval philosopher. Demonstrated Aristotelian reason and Christian faith are compatible. Received Aristotle through Averroes.

nodes.label: William of Ockham
nodes.description: Philosopher of Ockhams Razor. His nominalism anticipated empiricism and the scientific method.

nodes.label: Machiavelli
nodes.description: First modern political philosopher. The Prince is the founding document of political realism.

nodes.label: Francis Bacon
nodes.description: Father of the scientific method. Insisted on inductive reasoning from systematic observation and experiment.

nodes.label: Thomas Hobbes
nodes.description: Philosopher of the social contract. Without sovereign authority, life is solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short.

nodes.label: John Locke
nodes.description: The philosopher of liberty. Life, liberty, property. Directly shaped the American Declaration of Independence.

nodes.label: Montesquieu
nodes.description: Philosopher of separation of powers. His division of legislative, executive, judicial became foundation of constitutional democracy.

nodes.label: Voltaire
nodes.description: The great destroyer of superstition. Defining voice of the Enlightenment critical spirit.

nodes.label: David Hume
nodes.description: The most radical empiricist. Causation and moral facts cannot be derived from reason. Awoke Kant from dogmatic slumber.

nodes.label: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
nodes.description: Philosopher of the general will. His Social Contract provided ideological foundation for the French Revolution.

nodes.label: Immanuel Kant
nodes.description: The philosophers philosopher. Reconciled empiricism and rationalism. Categorical imperative grounded morality in reason.

nodes.label: Adam Smith
nodes.description: Father of modern economics. Free markets guided by self-interest produce social prosperity. A moral philosopher first.

nodes.label: Edmund Burke
nodes.description: Father of modern conservatism. Abstract rationalist principles applied to politics produce destruction.

nodes.label: Georg Hegel
nodes.description: Philosopher of history and dialectic. Reality is self-development of Absolute Spirit through contradiction and synthesis.

nodes.label: Arthur Schopenhauer
nodes.description: Philosopher of the will and pessimism. First major Western philosopher to engage Buddhism and Hinduism seriously.

nodes.label: Karl Marx
nodes.description: Most consequential political philosopher of modernity. His analysis became foundation of communist movements.

nodes.label: John Stuart Mill
nodes.description: The definitive liberal philosopher. His harm principle remains foundation of liberal political thought.

nodes.label: Trial of Socrates
nodes.description: Athens executed Socrates for impiety. The first philosopher martyred for ideas.

nodes.label: Conquests of Alexander
nodes.description: Alexander spread Greek culture across Persia, Egypt, and India. Philosophy became cosmopolitan.

nodes.label: Destruction of Nalanda
nodes.description: Destruction of the greatest center of Buddhist learning. End of classical Indian Buddhist philosophy.

nodes.label: Fall of Rome
nodes.description: Collapse of Western Roman Empire. Classical learning survived only in monasteries and the Islamic world.

nodes.label: Islamic Translation Movement
nodes.description: Systematic translation of Greek texts into Arabic. Without it, Aristotle would have been lost to the West.

nodes.label: Magna Carta
nodes.description: First formal limitation of royal power. Foundation of constitutional government.

nodes.label: Black Death
nodes.description: Plague killed one-third of Europe. Accelerated crisis of medieval theology.

nodes.label: Gutenberg Printing Press
nodes.description: Most transformative technology before the internet. Ideas spread faster than institutions could suppress.

nodes.label: Protestant Reformation
nodes.description: Luther challenged papal authority. Individual conscience against institutional authority.

nodes.label: Scientific Revolution
nodes.description: Copernicus, Galileo, Newton overturned 1,500 years of cosmology. Most consequential intellectual event in history.

nodes.label: American Revolution
nodes.description: First successful colonial revolution grounded in Lockean natural rights.

nodes.label: Communist Manifesto Published
nodes.description: Most politically consequential pamphlet in history.

nodes.label: World War I
nodes.description: Catastrophic collapse of liberal European order. Shattered Enlightenment faith in progress.

nodes.label: Russian Revolution
nodes.description: First successful Marxist revolution. Greatest disproof of utopian rationalism in history.

nodes.label: Rise of Nazism
nodes.description: Most catastrophic failure of a civilization to protect itself from ideas.

nodes.label: World War II
nodes.description: Deadliest conflict in human history. Permanently redefined the meaning of evil.

nodes.label: The Holocaust
nodes.description: Systematic murder of six million Jews. How rational bureaucracies produce industrialized mass murder.

nodes.label: Indian Independence
nodes.description: End of British rule achieved through non-violent resistance. Satyagraha defeated a global empire.

nodes.label: Cold War
nodes.description: Ideological confrontation between capitalism and communism. Greatest experiment in free markets vs central planning.

nodes.label: Chinese Cultural Revolution
nodes.description: Mao attempt to purge traditional culture. Most systematic philosophical self-destruction in history.

nodes.label: Reality vs Mysticism
nodes.description: Does an objective reality exist independent of consciousness, or is reality ultimately mental, spiritual, or unknowable? The foundational metaphysical battle.

nodes.label: Reason vs Faith
nodes.description: Is reason the only valid means of knowledge, or can faith, revelation, or intuition access truths beyond rational inquiry? The epistemological battle.

nodes.label: Individualism vs Collectivism
nodes.description: Is the individual the fundamental unit of moral and political value, or does the group — tribe, class, nation, race — take precedence? The political battle.

nodes.label: Freedom vs Coercion
nodes.description: Should human relationships be based on voluntary consent, or is force a legitimate tool for achieving social goals? The battle over the use of power.

nodes.label: Value Creation vs Nihilism
nodes.description: Are objective values possible, or is existence ultimately meaningless? Can humans create genuine meaning, or is all valuation arbitrary? The ethical battle.

nodes.label: Free Market vs Central Planning
nodes.description: Should economic activity be coordinated through voluntary exchange and price signals, or through central direction by authorities? The economic battle.

nodes.label: Beauty vs Chaos
nodes.description: Is beauty objective and discoverable, or is aesthetic value purely subjective? Does art require standards, or is transgression itself the point? The aesthetic battle.

nodes.label: Good vs Evil
nodes.description: The ultimate moral battle. Does good have a specific nature that can be identified and pursued? Or is morality relative, situational, or a mask for power?

nodes.label: Fall of the Berlin Wall
nodes.description: Symbolic end of Cold War. Appeared to confirm arguments against central planning and totalitarianism.

nodes.label: September 11 Attacks
nodes.description: Can liberal societies defend themselves without betraying their values?

nodes.label: 2008 Financial Crisis
nodes.description: Largest economic shock since Great Depression. Revived debates about capitalism stability.

nodes.label: AI Revolution
nodes.description: Forces philosophy to revisit questions about consciousness, creativity, and intelligence.

nodes.label: Reason
nodes.description: The faculty of logical inference from evidence. Central concept of Western philosophy. In tension with faith across every era.

nodes.label: Individual Rights
nodes.description: The principle that each person possesses inviolable freedoms that no collective may override. Foundation of liberalism.

nodes.label: Free Market
nodes.description: Voluntary exchange of goods and services without coercive interference. Defended by Smith, Hayek, and classical liberals.

nodes.label: Dialectic
nodes.description: Method of argument through contradiction. From Socratic dialogue to Hegelian synthesis to Marxist materialism.

nodes.label: Natural Law
nodes.description: Moral principles discoverable by reason and universal to all humans. From Stoics through Aquinas to Locke.

nodes.label: Social Contract
nodes.description: Theory that political authority derives from agreement among individuals. Hobbes, Locke, Rousseau, Rawls.

nodes.label: Ancient Greece
nodes.description: The birthplace of Western philosophy, democracy, and systematic rational inquiry.

nodes.label: Classical China
nodes.description: The era of the Hundred Schools of Thought — Confucianism, Taoism, Legalism, Mohism competing to define proper order.

nodes.label: Classical India
nodes.description: The era of the Upanishads, the Buddha, the Jains — competing visions of reality, consciousness, and liberation.

nodes.label: Islamic Golden Age
nodes.description: Extraordinary intellectual flourishing. Muslim philosophers preserved and extended Greek works. Without this era, Aristotle would have been lost.

nodes.label: Roman Empire
nodes.description: The political and cultural heir to Greece. Rome synthesized Greek philosophy with practical governance and Stoic ethics.

nodes.label: Enlightenment
nodes.description: The age of reason — systematic application of rational inquiry to science, politics, ethics, and religion.

nodes.label: 19th Century
nodes.description: The century of ideological crystallization. Liberalism, conservatism, socialism, nationalism emerged as distinct philosophies.

nodes.label: 20th Century
nodes.description: The most violent and transformative century. Philosophy confronted unprecedented evil and technological power.

nodes.label: Contemporary
nodes.description: The digital age — collapse of geographic limits, rise of AI, collision of global capitalism with authoritarian resurgence.

nodes.label: Axial Age
nodes.description: The pivotal period when the great intellectual traditions emerged simultaneously — Greek philosophy, Chinese Confucianism and Taoism, Indian Buddhism, and Hebrew monotheism.

nodes.label: Renaissance
nodes.description: The rebirth of classical learning. Thinkers returned to Greek and Roman sources, placing human reason at the center.

nodes.label: Middle Ages
nodes.description: A millennium in which theological authority dominated Western intellectual life. Philosophy survived within religious institutions.

nodes.label: Hellenistic Period
nodes.description: The era following Alexander when Greek culture spread across the known world. Stoicism, Epicureanism, and Skepticism emerged.

nodes.label: Siddhartha Gautama
nodes.description: The Buddha. Proposed anatta (no permanent self) and dependent origination. The most psychologically sophisticated ancient ethics.

nodes.label: Mahavira
nodes.description: The 24th Tirthankara of Jainism. Most rigorous philosophy of non-violence in history.

nodes.label: Nagarjuna
nodes.description: Founder of Madhyamaka Buddhism. Sunyata (emptiness) — all phenomena lack inherent existence.

nodes.label: Adi Shankara
nodes.description: Revived Advaita Vedanta. Brahman is the only reality; multiplicity is maya (illusion).

nodes.label: Mahatma Gandhi
nodes.description: Transformed Indian ethics into political practice. Satyagraha — non-violent resistance to coercion.

nodes.label: Averroes (Ibn Rushd)
nodes.description: The Commentator. His commentaries on Aristotle made Aquinas possible. Defended reason against Al-Ghazali.

nodes.label: Ibn Khaldun
nodes.description: Founder of sociology and philosophy of history. First systematic theory of how civilizations rise and fall.

nodes.label: Baruch Spinoza
nodes.description: Philosopher of God-or-Nature. His pantheism was the most radical system of the 17th century.

nodes.label: René Descartes
nodes.description: Father of modern philosophy. I think, therefore I am. Founded the mind-body problem.

nodes.label: Gottfried Leibniz
nodes.description: Co-inventor of calculus. Philosopher of pre-established harmony and monadology.

nodes.label: Mary Wollstonecraft
nodes.description: First systematic feminist philosopher. If reason is the standard of dignity, women possess it equally.

nodes.label: Søren Kierkegaard
nodes.description: The first existentialist. Insisted on irreducible reality of the individual facing absolute choices.

nodes.label: Friedrich Nietzsche
nodes.description: Philosopher of will to power and death of God. One of the most dangerous and most misread thinkers in history.

nodes.label: Bertrand Russell
nodes.description: Philosopher of logical atomism. Defense of reason against all forms of mysticism defined Anglo-American philosophy.

nodes.label: Ludwig Wittgenstein
nodes.description: Philosophized twice with contradictory conclusions. Transformed philosophy of language twice in one lifetime.

nodes.label: Martin Heidegger
nodes.description: Most controversial philosopher of 20th century. Being and Time transformed existentialism and phenomenology.

nodes.label: Karl Popper
nodes.description: Philosopher of science and open society. Falsifiability distinguishes science from pseudoscience.

nodes.label: Friedrich Hayek
nodes.description: Defender of spontaneous order. Central planning inevitably leads to tyranny. Defined classical liberalism.

nodes.label: Hannah Arendt
nodes.description: Philosopher of totalitarianism. Identified Nazism and Stalinism as qualitatively new forms of evil.

nodes.label: Jean-Paul Sartre
nodes.description: Philosopher of radical freedom and bad faith. Existence precedes essence.

nodes.label: John Rawls
nodes.description: Most influential political philosopher in Anglo-American academia. Veil of ignorance. A Theory of Justice.

nodes.label: Michel Foucault
nodes.description: Philosopher of power and knowledge. Analyzed how institutions shape human subjects through normalization.

nodes.label: Ayn Rand
nodes.description: Developed Objectivism. Reality exists independent of consciousness. Reason is the only valid means of knowledge. Rational self-interest is the proper standard of value.

nodes.label: French Revolution
nodes.description: Most consequential political event of modern era. Established popular sovereignty. Terror revealed catastrophic potential.

nodes.label: Industrial Revolution
nodes.description: Transformation through machinery and capital. Every major 19th century philosophy is a response to it.

nodes.label: Darwin Origin of Species
nodes.description: Most philosophically disruptive scientific discovery since Copernicus.

nodes.label: Categorical Imperative
nodes.description: Kant moral law: act only according to principles you could will to be universal laws.

nodes.label: Will to Power
nodes.description: Nietzsche concept of the fundamental drive underlying all human behavior. Self-overcoming through creative excellence.

nodes.label: Dasein
nodes.description: Heidegger term for human existence as being-in-the-world. Transformed existentialism and phenomenology.

nodes.label: Falsifiability
nodes.description: Popper criterion: a theory is scientific only if it can be proven false. Distinguishes science from pseudoscience.

nodes.label: Spontaneous Order
nodes.description: Hayek concept: complex social institutions emerge from human action but not human design. Markets, language, law.

nodes.label: Objectivism
nodes.description: Rand philosophy: objective reality, reason as only valid knowledge, rational self-interest, individual rights, laissez-faire capitalism.

links.label: Thales initiated natural philosophy; Heraclitus developed it into flux doctrine.
Thales question about fundamental substance prompted Parmenides monist answer.
Heraclitean flux drove Plato to seek unchanging Forms beyond the sensible world.
Parmenides unchanging Being becomes the model for Plato eternal Forms.
Pythagorean mathematics and mysticism profoundly shaped Platonic metaphysics.
Socrates method and martyrdom made Plato a philosopher.
Aristotle studied under Plato for twenty years, then rejected his idealism.
Aquinas synthesized Aristotelian philosophy with Christian theology.
Averroes commentaries transmitted Aristotle to the medieval world.
Rand identified Aristotle as her primary philosophical ancestor.
Plotinus developed Plato into systematic Neoplatonism.
Augustine converted Neoplatonism into Christian theology.
Epicurus adopted and developed Democritean atomism.
Marcus Aurelius practiced Stoicism under real conditions of imperial power.
Cicero transmitted Stoic ethics and natural law to Rome.
Al-Kindi initiated Islamic philosophy; Al-Farabi systematized it.
Avicenna built on Al-Farabi Aristotelian synthesis.
Al-Ghazali attacked the Avicennan philosophical tradition.
Averroes wrote Incoherence of the Incoherence to defend philosophy against Al-Ghazali.
Aquinas received Aristotle through Averroes commentaries.
Aquinas synthesized Augustinian theology with Aristotelian philosophy.
Ockham nominalism attacked Thomistic realism.
Ockham emphasis on particulars anticipated empiricism.
Translation movement made Greek philosophy available in Arabic.
Without translations, Averroes commentaries would have been impossible.
Rome collapse began the medieval period in Western Europe.
Printing enabled rapid spread of Renaissance ideas.
Luther ideas spread through printed pamphlets.
Scientific method became model for Enlightenment rational inquiry.
Bacon empiricism shaped Locke epistemology.
Locke social contract answered Hobbes absolutism.
Rousseau transformed Hobbes social contract into general will.
Montesquieu developed Locke separation of powers.
Declaration of Independence directly applied Lockean rights.
Locke property theory grounded Smith economics.
French revolutionaries invoked Rousseau general will.
Kant credited Rousseau with teaching him to respect common humanity.
Hume skepticism awoke Kant from dogmatic slumber.
Hume and Smith were close friends; Hume shaped Smith moral philosophy.
Hegel developed and transformed Kantian idealism.
Schopenhauer built on Kant thing-in-itself as blind Will.
Smith Wealth of Nations is foundation of free market economics.
Hayek deepened Smith invisible hand into epistemological argument.
Burke conservatism influenced Hayek critique of rationalist planning.
Marx inverted Hegel dialectic from idealism to materialism.
Kierkegaard existentialism was direct reaction against Hegelian system.
Nietzsche began as Schopenhauer disciple before rejecting pessimism.
Bolsheviks claimed to implement Marxist revolution.
Marx wrote the Communist Manifesto.
Heidegger extensive engagement with Nietzsche shaped his later work.
Foucault genealogical method derived from Nietzsche.
Kierkegaard existentialism directly influenced Being and Time.
Sartre existentialism built on Kierkegaard individual existence.
Rawls liberalism developed from Mill utilitarian tradition.
Russell supervised Wittgenstein at Cambridge.
Popper developed philosophy partly in response to Wittgenstein.
Popper and Hayek were allies defending open society against totalitarianism.
Fall of communism appeared to vindicate Hayek critique of central planning.
Arendt analyzed the Holocaust and coined banality of evil.
Zhuangzi developed Laozi Taoism into literary and philosophical masterpiece.
Mencius systematized Confucian ethics and added doctrine of innate goodness.
Han Fei Legalism was partly reaction against Confucian virtue ethics.
Mao Cultural Revolution explicitly targeted Confucian tradition.
Han Fei incorporated Mohist consequentialism into Legalist framework.
Taoist naturalness opposed Confucian social ritual and hierarchy.
Buddha responded to Upanishadic Atman with doctrine of anatta.
Shankara systematized Upanishadic non-dualism into Advaita Vedanta.
Nagarjuna developed Buddha anatta into systematic emptiness philosophy.
Schopenhauer was first major Western philosopher to engage Buddhism seriously.
Gandhi ahimsa drew directly on Jain tradition of non-violence.
Gandhi satyagraha achieved Indian independence without war.
Classical China produced Confucian tradition.
Classical India produced Buddhist philosophy.
Axial Age encompassed Chinese philosophical flowering.
Axial Age encompassed Indian philosophical flowering.
Axial Age encompassed Greek philosophical flowering.
Aristotle grounded philosophy in observable reality against Platonic other-worldliness.
Plato Forms privilege abstract ideals over physical reality.
Aquinas attempted synthesis of Aristotelian reason and Christian faith.
Al-Ghazali attack on philosophy represented victory of faith over reason in Islam.
Averroes defended reason against Al-Ghazali — the road not taken.
Locke established individual rights as foundation of political philosophy.
Marx subordinated individual to class and historical process.
Rand defended individual rights against all forms of collectivism.
Smith established philosophical case for free markets.
Hayek deepened case for markets as epistemological necessity.
Nietzsche confronted nihilism and proposed value creation through will to power.
Sartre existentialism attempted to create meaning in meaningless universe.
Holocaust forced philosophy to confront radical evil.
Soviet experiment tested central planning against markets.
Cold War was global contest between freedom and totalitarian coercion.
Kant formulated the categorical imperative as foundation of rational ethics.
Nietzsche developed will to power as fundamental drive.
Heidegger coined Dasein as term for human existence.
Popper proposed falsifiability as criterion of science.
Hayek developed spontaneous order as alternative to planning.
Rand developed Objectivism as integrated philosophical system.
Locke formulated natural rights to life, liberty, property.
Locke developed social contract based on consent.
Hobbes originated modern social contract theory.
Cicero transmitted Stoic natural law to Western tradition.
Aquinas synthesized natural law with Christian theology.
Socratic method established dialectic as philosophical practice.
Hegel transformed dialectic into engine of historical development.
Reason is the central concept in the battle against faith.
Individual rights are foundation of individualism against collectivism.
Free market concept anchors the battle against central planning.
